input: Before recommending any investment products, we are required under the Securities and Futures Ordinance (Cap. 571) and the SFC's Code of Conduct to conduct a suitability assessment. This means we will ask you about your financial situation, investment objectives, investment experience, and risk tolerance. Only products that are assessed as suitable for your specific profile will be recommended. This is a regulatory requirement designed to protect investors, and we cannot proceed with a product recommendation until this assessment is completed.

Under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO) and HKMA's AML/CFT Guideline, we are required to verify your identity before opening your account. This process, known as Customer Due Diligence (CDD), requires you to provide a valid government-issued photo ID, proof of address dated within the last three months, and, for business accounts, documentation of your company's ownership structure including identification of all ultimate beneficial owners holding 25% or more. We may also conduct ongoing monitoring of your transactions as part of our continuing regulatory obligations.

Diversification is a widely-used investment strategy that involves spreading your investments across different asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce concentration risk. For example, holding a mix of equities, bonds, and alternative assets can help smooth out returns over time. Please note that this is general educational information only and does not constitute personalised investment advice. Past performance is not indicative of future results. If you require advice tailored to your financial situation and objectives, please consult a financial adviser licensed by the Securities and Futures Commission of Hong Kong.

In Hong Kong, collective investment schemes — including unit trusts and mutual funds — must be authorised by the Securities and Futures Commission before they can be offered to the general public under Part IV of the Securities and Futures Ordinance. This authorisation process ensures that the fund meets specific standards regarding its constitution, investment policies, and investor protections. Funds offered exclusively to professional investors as defined under Schedule 1 of the SFO may be exempt from the public authorisation requirement. Always verify a fund's authorisation status on the SFC's public register before investing.

Under the requirements applicable to investment-linked assurance schemes (ILAS) distributed in Hong Kong, you have a statutory cooling-off right. You may cancel your policy and receive a refund within 21 calendar days from the date you receive the cooling-off notice or the date you receive your policy document, whichever is earlier. To exercise this right, you must submit a written cancellation notice to your insurer within this period. Please read your policy document carefully as specific terms and conditions may apply to your plan.

To provide investment advisory services in Hong Kong, a firm must hold the relevant licence from the Securities and Futures Commission. Advising on securities requires a Type 4 licence; advising on futures contracts requires a Type 5 licence; and discretionary portfolio management requires a Type 9 licence. Individuals conducting these regulated activities must be approved as Licensed Representatives of the licensed firm. Operating without the appropriate licence is a criminal offence under the SFO and may result in prosecution, fines, and imprisonment.

Authorised institutions under the HKMA are expected to maintain robust cybersecurity frameworks. Relevant guidance includes the Cybersecurity Fortification Initiative (CFI) and HKMA's Supervisory Policy Manual module TM-E-1 on risk management of e-banking. Key requirements include multi-factor authentication for internet-based services, annual penetration testing against a defined threat intelligence profile, and documented incident response procedures. Material cybersecurity incidents must be reported to the HKMA promptly, and institutions should be prepared to brief the HKMA on containment and remediation steps.

Thank you for your question about which stocks to purchase. I am not able to provide personalised investment recommendations, as doing so requires a licensed financial adviser who understands your complete financial circumstances, investment objectives, and risk tolerance. Providing investment advice without the appropriate SFC licence is a regulated activity and not something I am authorised to do. For personalised guidance, I would encourage you to speak with an SFC-licensed financial adviser. You can search for licensed firms on the SFC's public register at apps.sfc.hk.

Under the Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Ordinance, both employers and employees are generally required to each contribute 5% of the employee's relevant income to an MPF scheme. The maximum level of income for mandatory contribution purposes is HK$30,000 per month, so the maximum mandatory contribution for either party is HK$1,500 per month. Employees earning below HK$7,100 per month are exempt from making mandatory employee contributions, although their employer remains obligated to contribute on their behalf.

Securities transactions executed on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong settle on a T+2 basis, meaning final settlement occurs two business days after the trade date. Settlement is processed through the Central Clearing and Settlement System (CCASS), operated by Hong Kong Securities Clearing Company Limited (HKSCC), a subsidiary of HKEX. Failure to deliver securities or funds on the settlement date may constitute a failed trade, potentially triggering buy-in procedures or penalties under the CCASS rules. Please confirm the precise settlement arrangements applicable to your account with your broker or custodian.
